source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du

extracts.extract: DJ builds on the older practices of CAR and takes them further by engaging with data analysts, infographic designers and visualizers in producing and communicating stories via multimedia platforms.
extracts.notes: Citing Yang and Du 2016; positions DJ as inherently collaborative across analyst/designer/journalist roles

extracts.extract: Howard (2014) defined DJ as 'gathering, cleaning, organizing, analysing, visualizing, and publishing data to support the creation of acts of journalism'.
extracts.notes: Canonical pipeline definition from Howard 2014; maps directly to Get-Clean-Analyse-Present steps

extracts.extract: Most theorists agree data skill sets include accessing data, analysing data and developing stories on the basis of data analysis, which are beyond traditional journalistic skills like conducting interviews and writing reports.
extracts.notes: Citing Treadwell et al. 2016; frames DJ skills as additive to traditional journalism, not replacing it

extracts.extract: Some data journalists began their career as graphic designers or computer scientists rather than reporters. Based on their educational background, data journalists' development paths can be summarized into two categories: the journalism path, which is based on a journalistic background complemented by technological skills, and the technology path, which contributes to news production from the starting point of offering technological support.
extracts.notes: Two-path model (journalism-to-tech vs tech-to-journalism); citing Fink and Anderson 2015

extracts.extract: Students' low levels of mathematical and quantitative literacy skills made teaching DJ difficult.
extracts.notes: Quoting Davies and Cullen 2016 survey of 25 Australian universities; 11 of 25 cited this barrier

extracts.extract: Data acquisition, analysis and visualization are frequently mentioned typical skills that data journalists should master.
extracts.notes: Authors' synthesis citing Gray et al. 2012, Karlsen and Stavelin 2014, Treadwell et al. 2016, Yang and Du 2016

extracts.extract: Significant differences exist in data analysis and data visualization. J&C majors and science majors show no difference in identifying data.
extracts.notes: Finding tables: data analysis t=-3.37 p<0.01, visualization t=-2.12 p<0.05, identifying data not significant. Science majors outperform on analysis/viz but not on finding data.

extracts.extract: The biggest difficulty is that the students have no foundation in this field and without prior knowledge, they had nothing to connect to and understand the new subject.
extracts.notes: Instructor quote; implies DJ education needs prerequisite scaffolding before technical skills

extracts.extract: Many programmers told me it's easy to master a code language, but you will give up at the first step — environment configuration. Without the configuration, you cannot install the software or the software cannot run well.
extracts.notes: Alumnus A3 quote; environment configuration as a hidden barrier to entry for journalists learning technical tools

extracts.extract: Actually my course was focused on data visualization. However, since many students had no background of data journalism, I had to supplement the content with the workflow of data journalism production.
extracts.notes: Instructor quote; teaching visualization in isolation fails without upstream pipeline context

extracts.extract: The efforts the students made after class, the practice they had, led to differences among students. The excellent students in my class all spent a lot of time exploring more after class—they even gave assignments to themselves—while there were also students who just reached the minimum requirements of the homework.
extracts.notes: Instructor quote; self-assigned practice distinguishes high performers in technical skill acquisition

extracts.extract: Using data to tell news stories is a team effort in which reporting meets programming and involves reporters, editors, programmers, graphic artists, web designers and app developers. One opinion is that for journalists, the aim of mastering programming languages is to empower them to create stories for digital platforms with the same precision as traditional media, so they do not need to become an expert in computer coding.
extracts.notes: Citing Doherty 2012; argues journalists need functional not expert-level coding; DJ as team sport

extracts.extract: It is not easy for students to get a clear study goal. When you become a worker, with a clear goal to solve the problems you meet in your daily work, your study can be more efficient. More interactions with the industry, such as inviting practitioners to deliver lectures or visiting newsrooms, can help learners have a concrete understanding of DJ and make their learning more goal oriented.
extracts.notes: Alumna quote plus authors' synthesis; real-world problem framing accelerates DJ skill acquisition

extracts.extract: Science majors and journalism majors have complementary skills in DJ production and can help each other solve problems. Cooperation between them can enhance learning efficiency.
extracts.notes: Key finding: mixed-discipline teams outperform single-discipline cohorts in DJ training

extracts.extract: Another way to help students deal with the difficulties is to engage them in realistic, problem-centred activities. The visible outcomes, such as building a website, could bring students confidence and a sense of achievement. These instant and apparent rewards can motivate learners to achieve more.
extracts.notes: Visible deliverables as pedagogical strategy; applicable to newsroom training design

extracts.extract: It is unnecessary to bring all the knowledge and skills involved in the training of computer science and journalism into the training of DJ. In fact, using data for news storytelling requires only a very basic set of coding and programming skills. Moreover, for the interdisciplinary educational materials to be organically absorbed by the learners, they should be organized into a logical and systematic structure, in order to effectively guide the students towards the integration of multiple subject areas step by step.
extracts.notes: Authors' key recommendation: pare skills to minimum viable set and sequence them logically

extracts.extract: To avoid the polarity problem, both discipline-based and interdisciplinary experiences should be taken as essential in the curriculum design. In terms of the discipline-based approach, students should be equipped with the necessary knowledge and skills of their own major, be it journalism, computer science, visual design or others. As for interdisciplinary experiences, it can be achieved through in-depth interdisciplinary interactions, such as practical case studies, team projects, field trips to the industry as well as exchange visits with other schools and departments.
extracts.notes: Citing Jacobs 1989 polarity problem; balance domain depth with cross-domain integration activities

extracts.extract: The instructor observed that in the short term it is easier to enter DJ via the technology path.
extracts.notes: Practitioner observation; technologists learn journalism faster than journalists learn technology, at least initially

extracts.extract: If several students could form a learning group to encourage each other, they could do some projects based on what they have learnt in class. In this way, they can practise more.
extracts.notes: Small-group project work as mechanism to multiply practice time outside class

extracts.extract: One cannot master any kind of coding language in a short time. The learning process always makes people frustrated.
extracts.notes: Alumnus A2 quote; frustration tolerance as prerequisite for technical skill development in journalism
